body_txt: A soon-to-be-married woman has difficulties with the idea of changing her last name. Or perhaps, her soon-to-be husband has difficulties, or maybe his mother has problems (often because she doesn't like any daughter-in-law taking the same name she changed her own to). This trope usually starts with the woman in question writing out her maiden name along with her married name to try it out, or try saying it in front of a mirror for practice. Usually, the lady is an established career woman (or wants to be). Less often, there is the issue of the aesthetics of the name: should Rhea Mills have to be saddled with ridicule for the rest of her life for having married Robert Peer? (As a general rule, since you never know who she might marry, it would be wise to avoid naming your daughter "Anita".) Truth in Television , obviously, and a potentially contentious issue. This trope is going to be most prevalent in cultures descended from The British Empire and The French Colonial Empire , because that's where the practice of the wife taking her husband's name originated in the early modern period. It was a way to give the husband firm legal control of any property the wife brought with her (except some jewelry) and to indicate that her identity had passed from her father's family (and ownership) to her husband's. Thus, in addition to many other personal reasons for maintaining your maiden name, it has also been a trend in feminism (whether the author is pro- or anti-) as a method for opposing traditions of patriarchy. Other cultures have other naming conventions, and this debate may never happen or be fairly unremarkable as just a practical joke. Historically, of course, most people didn't even have surnames, so this trope could never arise. A traditional solution was for a married woman to take her maiden name as a middle name (e.g. Laura Ingalls Wilder ). It's still done today, as was the case with Robin Wright when she was married to Sean Penn (well... maybe that's not the most relevant case ). Note that this is different from the more modern solution of a hyphenated name. Robin Wright Penn is not the same as Robin Wright-Penn, and this can cause quite a bit of annoyance as hyphenated names gain more prominence over time, while the "maiden name as middle name" falls out of favor. Particularly applies if you're a media personality. This is partly due to working in an industry where your name is your brand, and partly due to the typical brevity of celebrity marriages. Many female performers split the difference by using their maiden name as their stage name while taking their husband's surname for legal purposes. For instance, Sarah Michelle Gellar is officially Sarah Michelle Prinze . Naturally, this trope only applies in countries/cultures where the wife traditionally takes her husband's last name upon marriage. Whether a married woman reverts to her maiden name after marriage usually depends on how it ends. If she's widowed, she'll usually keep the married name unless and until she remarries. If she's divorced, she may go back to her maiden name, but she might not if the married name is tied to her career or she still thinks kindly on the marriage while acknowledging it needed to end. One example that comes to mind is Phylicia Rashad , who divorced Ahmad in 2001 but has kept the name, both personally and professionally. With same-sex marriage becoming legal in many parts of the world these days, the problem with applying this practice to a same-sex couple is obvious. Since there is no traditional protocol to follow, it's up to each couple to choose a solution that works for them. The most common solutions are for the spouses to simply keep their original names or to hyphenate. It's not unheard of for one partner to take the other's name, but it may lead to the unwelcome perception that the one who changed their name is the "wife" in the relationship. Between same-sex marriage and the increasing frequency of men taking their wives' names instead of vice versa , there has been the growing question of how a married man should refer to his unmarried name if applicable. The term "bachelor name" has been coined as the Spear Counterpart to a woman's maiden name, but is not in common use. See also Meaningful Rename . Compare Nom de Mom , where someone changed their name back to their mother's original name. Also see the Mrs. Hypothetical , who's gone through this debate well in advance, and Took the Wife's Name , where the husband changes his name to the wife's instead.
